name: ALT EFF Film Club x GuruJal Gurugram | 17th July
description: A brilliant selection of cinematic, poetic, diverse pieces of environmental storytelling that are short enough to get you hooked and never long enough to bore!Would You Still Love Me If I Was A Sticky Frog?SheraPapillon (Butterfly) All activities, including discussions and refreshments, will occur between 7:00 and 9:00 PM. What's Included?Exclusive screenings of the world's best environmental cinemaAccess to a community of environmentally inclined, film-loving peopleA chance to express yourself at ALT EFF VoicesPost-film discussions.About Film Club: If you are tired of the outrage cycle, doomscrolling collapse, and the looming AI takeover narrative. If you have caught yourself going numb just to cope. We see you.All Living Things Environmental Film Festival is back with the third season of Film Club. A monthly meet up across 12 cities over 5 months, orbiting environmental cinema that cracks you open without trapping you in doom and gloom.It is a room full of people choosing to show up in cities whose infrastructure persuades you to stay home, choosing to sit together, watch together, and turn private anxiety into collective imagination. Messy. Human. Honest conversation. With snacks. Always snacks.

location.address: 6/9, F-2, Block F, DLF Phase 1, Sector 26A, Gurugram, Haryana 122002, India

name: The Procrastireaders : Books & Coffee Tasting
description: The Procrastireaders: Books & Coffee TastingOverviewA slow reading circle designed around comfort, not pressure.People come together to read, exchange thoughts, and return to books in a way that feels lighter and more approachable. Stories, shared slowly.If you have been wanting to get back to reading but find it hard to make the time at home, this is for you. The Procrastireaders is a dedicated space to finally open that book you have been meaning to finish. It is a slow paced morning designed for quiet reading and great coffee in the company of others who are doing the same.What is Stories of Hope?Stories of Hope is a community built around a simple idea, that people need places where they can slow down and be heard. We create small, intentional spaces for writing, listening, creating, and reflecting. There is no expectation to perform and no pressure to have the right words. Just a space to pause and be with what is already there.What to expectBring Your Own Book: Whether it is a novel you started months ago or a new non fiction find, bring along whatever you are currently reading.Dedicated Reading Time: Enjoy a block of quiet, uninterrupted time to sink into your book without the distractions of chores or your phone.Shared Atmosphere: The comfort of reading alongside a small group of people who are also prioritizing their love for books.Coffee & Chat: A low pressure chance to talk about what you are reading (or why you have been procrastinating) over a coffee at Unlocked.Details🗓️ Date: 18th July (Saturday)⏰ Time: 11:30 AM📍 Location: Sushant Lok Phase 1🎟️ Contribution: ₹700 (Includes Coffee)

We will unpack the small, often invisible digital survival strategies we have all built for ourselves, and explore what they reveal about how safe (or unsafe) our online spaces really are – and who is responsible for making them better.No prior knowledge needed, just you, your habits, and zero judgment :)

name: uL: Beyond The Tiger; Mega Mammal Futures In India
description: Join us for a lecture that asks a question India rarely pauses to consider:How do a billion people share a country with 3,600 tigers, 900 lions, and 28,000 elephants?About the LectureIn 1972, India staged a quiet coup. The lion — regal, heraldic, national symbol since independence — was replaced by the tiger as national animal. Decades later, the elephant was declared a national heritage animal. States claimed their own: the lion for Gujarat, the rhino for Assam.These are political gestures. But behind them lies a far older and more complicated story.For centuries, India's mega mammals have been revered and feared, worshipped and avoided. They have been symbols of power and objects of terror. And today, every one of them occupies a fraction of the range it held just two hundred years ago.The usual story here is one of gloom and decline. But this lecture takes a different path. Drawing on history, ecology, and the messy overlap between human and animal worlds, it asks not just what went wrong — but what went right. How have states and societies related to these enormous animals across centuries? When and why did conflicts become truly intense? And is there ground for hope?The past cannot give easy answers. But by showing us how we arrived at this juncture, it can equip us to think more clearly about the choices ahead.About the SpeakerMahesh Rangarajan teaches History and Environmental Studies at Ashoka University, Sonipat. He studied at Hindu College, University of Delhi, and at Balliol and Nuffield Colleges, Oxford, where he was a Rhodes Scholar. He has taught at Jadavpur, Cornell, Delhi, and Krea Universities, and at the National Centre for Biological Sciences, Bengaluru.His books include Fencing the Forest (1996), India's Wildlife History (2001), and Nature and Nation (2015). His edited works include The Oxford Anthology of Indian Wildlife (1999), and recent co-edited volumes Nature Contested (2026) and India's Forests (2026).He is fascinated by film music of the '60s and '70s, and rock — and yes, he finds ways to relate these to animals, history, and ecology.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

location.address: M9, Level, block market, 2, M, Block M, Greater Kailash II, Greater Kailash, New Delhi, Delhi 110048, India

name: uL: One Ring To Rule Them All
description: Join us for a lecture that reveals the one thing connecting ocean tides, black hole collisions, and the ground beneath your feet:Waves.About the LectureWhy does the rhythm of the ocean mirror the behaviour of subatomic particles? How can we "hear" the collision of black holes billions of light-years away — and what does that tell us about the ground we're standing on?The same question, asked at different scales.This lecture is a tour of the physical world through its most fundamental language: wave mechanics — the hidden thread that weaves through the fabric of reality. From the simple beauty of waves on a beach to the complex patterns of our changing climate, from sound and light to the quantum fields that make up existence itself, we'll journey across scales of time and space to discover something extraordinary.The universe does not just contain waves. It is composed of them.The session explores:How a single mathematical idea has the potential to connect phenomena across wildly different scalesWhat waves on a beach, seismic tremors, and gravitational waves have in commonHow we detect events happening billions of light-years away — by listeningWhy quantum mechanics tells us that at its deepest level, reality itself oscillatesAbout the SpeakerSuprit Singh is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics at IIT Delhi. A theoretical and observational physicist, his research spans classical and quantum gravity, cosmology, astrophysical magnetic fields, and the solar corona.He completed his doctorate at IUCAA Pune in 2014 and held fellowships as a SERB Overseas and MITACS Accelerate Fellow at the University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, before joining the IIT Delhi faculty.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Open SketchBook
description: Open SketchBookOverviewA slower way of seeing what’s already there.People gather outdoors to observe, sketch, and spend time noticing details that often go unseen. No pressure. Just attention.Our core belief is that you do not need to leave the city to feel grounded. In a world where everyone is distracted, we pause as a group to give our attention to one shared thing. Sketching becomes the medium to notice small, living details in urban spaces that we usually rush through.You do not need to be an artist to enjoy sketching. Open SketchBook is a session for anyone who wants to explore drawing as a way to relax and observe the world. It is about the process of putting pencil to paper rather than the final masterpiece.What is Stories of Hope?Stories of Hope is a community built around a simple idea, that people need places where they can slow down and be heard. We create small, intentional spaces for writing, listening, creating, and reflecting. There is no expectation to perform and no pressure to have the right words. Just a space to pause and be with what is already there.What to expectGuided Observation: Learn simple ways to look at objects or spaces and translate them into lines and shapes.Creative Freedom: A judgment free zone where you can experiment with sketching without worrying about "doing it right."Tactile Experience: Use your new diary and pencil kit to capture what you see in the moment.Relaxed Environment: Enjoy a late afternoon at Greenr with a small group of people who just want to create and connect.Details🗓️ Date: 18th July (Saturday)⏰ Time: 5:30 PM📍 Location: Greenr, Golf Course Ext Road🎟️ Contribution: ₹700 (Includes an A6 diary)

name: Safdarjung Tomb: Night Heritage Walk
description: Safdarjung’s Tomb Night Heritage Walk(curated by Darwesh heritage walks in collaboration with Sabhyata Foundation)Join us for a journey into the fading glow of Mughal splendour at Safdarjung's Tomb — often called the *last flicker of Mughal grandeur* in Delhi.Built in 1754 for Safdarjung, the powerful Nawab of Awadh and Prime Minister of the Mughal court, this monument stands at the twilight of an empire — grand in ambition, yet carrying within it the quiet signs of decline.Its story is as fascinating as its form. From courtly intrigues and shifting loyalties to the architectural choices that reveal a changing political landscape, Safdarjung’s life opens a compelling window into 18th-century Delhi.We will also trace a lesser-known layer of its history — how this tomb was constructed by reusing material from a pre-existing Mughal structure, quite literally building one memory over another.To truly understand Delhi — its rise, its resilience, and its gradual transitions — a visit to Safdarjung’s Tomb is essential.Come, walk with us through the final chapter of Mughal architectural brilliance — where grandeur meets gravity, and history lingers in every arch.

name: Bapus's Table 5th Edition by Bapu's Curries
description: Bapu's Curries is the story of a man who spent a lifetime collecting memories through food. Umesh Khaitan, affectionately known as Bapu, is a lawyer by profession but a cook, traveller and storyteller at heart. For decades, he has explored India's regional cuisines, travelled in search of ingredients, documented family traditions, and created recipes that celebrate both heritage and experimentation. Every dish in the book carries a memory—of his parents, his travels, his family, or a simple moment in the kitchen. Co-authored by his daughter Shreeparna Khaitan and culinary collaborator Surbhi Anand, Bapu's Curries became a way of preserving these stories for generations to come. But we soon realised that some stories are best experienced around a table rather than read on a page. That thought gave birth to Bapu's Table—an intimate dining experience where guests come together to savour thoughtfully curated menus inspired by the book, while sharing conversations, memories and the joy of slowing down over a meal. Over the last four editions, Bapu's Table has grown into a community of people who arrive as strangers and leave having shared stories over food. On 18th July, we are delighted to host the 5th Edition of Bapu's Table, a celebration of the many moods of the mango—taking India's most-loved summer fruit beyond the familiar and weaving it through every course in Bapu's signature style. We would love for you to join us around the table and become a part of the story.

name: Nature Walk - Kamla Nehru Ridge
description: Step into one of Delhi’s oldest and most fascinating green spaces with our guided Nature Walk at Kamla Nehru Ridge. Part of the ancient Aravalli range, this beautiful forest is often called the “Green Lungs of Delhi” and offers a unique blend of biodiversity, history, and peaceful walking trails. Participants will explore native flora, observe a variety of birds, butterflies, and other urban wildlife, and discover the ecological importance of the Ridge in maintaining Delhi’s environment. Along the way, we’ll also uncover stories of historic landmarks connected to the 1857 uprising, making this walk a perfect mix of nature, heritage, and learning. Whether you’re a beginner or a nature enthusiast, this experience promises a refreshing escape into the wild heart of the city. The walk will be conducted by Verhean Khanna.

name: Overthinkers Anonymous - Relationships Edition
description: Before You BlameOverviewA space for the relationships you carry but rarely talk about. People gather to finally say the things they've held back from a parent, a partner, a coworker, an in-law, anyone they can't avoid and can't quite be honest with. Said out loud, finally.Most relationship conflicts are not just about what happened; they are about how we interpret what happened. This session focuses on exploring how our thoughts shape our connections and why friction often feels more intense than the situation warrants. "Before You Blame" is a reflective workshop designed to help you look at your assumptions and inner patterns, moving you from reactivity toward steadiness and emotional responsibility.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community-led initiative dedicated to creating safe and mindful spaces. Through intimate workshops and gatherings, we help individuals navigate life’s complexities, from mental well-being to personal growth, with empathy and shared human connection.What to ExpectPattern Recognition: Explore how common emotional triggers and ego-driven patterns shape conflicts in marriage, family, or work.Internal Focus: Instead of trying to fix others, we gently shift the focus inward to understand our own interpretations and responses.Practical Tools: Learn actionable ways to question stressful thoughts and practice perspective shifts using real-life scenarios.Safe Exploration: A supportive, non-judgmental environment for honest discussion. This is a space for learning and reflection, not a lecture or therapy.Key TakeawaysClarity: Gain a deeper understanding of recurring relationship dynamics.Responsiveness: Walk away with tools to help you respond thoughtfully instead of reacting impulsively.Emotional Ownership: Build the internal steadiness needed to navigate life without requiring others to change first.Details🗓️ Date: 12th July (Sunday)⏰ Time: 11:30 AM📍 Location: Joaah Cafe

name: Bushcraft Workshop
description: 🌲 Bushcraft Workshop: Unleash Your Inner Wilderness Survivor at Forest Spirit Learning, Gurgaon! 🔥Get ready for an unforgettable adventure into the heart of nature!Join us for a Bushcraft Workshop at Forest Spirit Learning in Gurgaon — a thrilling 4-hour immersion where you’ll master essential survival skills, reconnect with the wild, and have a blast doing it. Perfect for adventure seekers of all ages — kids, families, teens, and adults — this program turns the forest into your ultimate playground.Why This Workshop Will Be Epic:We’ll keep it intimate and high-quality with a maximum of just 10 participants so everyone gets personalized guidance from experienced instructors.No crowds, just pure focus on fun, learning, and memorable moments in the great outdoors.Highlights of the Adventure:– Master Fire Starting — Learn ancient techniques to spark a roaring bonfire from scratch (even in tricky conditions!).– Pure Water Mastery — Filter and purify water like a true survivor.– Shelter Building — Pitch a tent and construct emergency natural shelters.– Knot Mastery — Tie essential knots that hold strong in any situation.– Emergency Signalling — Master ways to call for help and stay safe.– Bonfire Feast — Cook delicious potatoes right in the glowing embers — nothing tastes better than forest-cooked food!– First Aid Basics — Quick, practical skills to handle minor injuries in the wild.Additional Exciting Activities We’ll Include:– Foraging for safe, edible plants and natural resources.– Wildlife tracking and reading nature’s signs.– Building simple tools or natural cordage from the environment.– Basic navigation using the sun, stars, and landmarks.By the end, you’ll feel empowered, confident, and deeply connected to the forest!Duration: 4 hours of pure excitement.  Location: Forest Spirit Learning, Gurgaon.Refreshments: We’ll provide light snacks, drinks, and post-activity treats to keep your energy high.  What to Bring: Comfortable outdoor clothes, sturdy shoes, a water bottle, sunscreen/insect repellent, and your sense of adventure.We strongly encourage you to bring your own survival bag/kit if you have one — we’ll review and enhance it together!Essential Items for Your Survival Bag/KitHere’s a recommended starter list for a compact, effective bushcraft/survival kit (pack light but smart):– Fire-starting tools — Lighter/matches, ferro rod, tinder (cotton balls, dry bark, etc.).– Knife — A sturdy fixed-blade bushcraft knife (with sheath).– Water purification — Tablets, filter straw, or small pot for boiling.– Cordage — Paracord or natural rope (at least 10-15 meters).– Shelter — Lightweight tarp, emergency blanket, or small tent.– First Aid — Basic kit with bandages, antiseptic, tweezers, pain relief, and personal meds.– Navigation — Compass, small map/whistle, signal mirror.– Multi-tool– Food/Water — Energy bars, reusable container.– Extra — Headlamp/flashlight with batteries, gloves, notepad/pen, trash bag (leave no trace!).We’ll provide a full printable checklist when you register.Pricing₹1,500 per person (including all materials, instruction, refreshments, and a take-home bushcraft tip sheet).This pricing reflects our commitment to quality instruction, safe equipment, and a premium intimate experience in a beautiful natural setting.Early bird or group discounts may apply for families registering together.Spots are strictly limited to 10 — secure yours now before they vanish!This is more than just a workshop; it’s a transformative adventure that will leave you inspired, skilled, and craving more time in the wild.Ready to awaken your inner survivor?Forest Spirit Learning – Where Nature Teaches Best. 🌿🔥See you under the trees! 🏕️

location.address: 9X6P+79, Sector 79, Gurugram, Haryana 122012, India

name: #lecture35: Why Women Don't Orgasm
description: Why is the clitoris still treated like a mysterious bonus feature when it is literally the only human organ designed purely for pleasure?In Why Women Don't Orgasm (And Whose Fault Is It, Anyway?): A Live Investigation into Sex Ed, the Clitoris, and 3,000 Years of Bad Information, Dr Tanaya (better known online as Dr Cuterus) takes us on a funny, furious, and fascinating journey through the history of how women’s pleasure got ignored, misunderstood, medicalised, shamed, and occasionally just… erased from the textbooks (literally).This isn’t a lecture about awkward conversations.It’s a lecture about how knowledge is created, how misinformation survives for centuries, and why asking better questions can fundamentally change the way we understand ourselves.Whether you’re interested in science, medicine, psychology, history, gender, or simply enjoy questioning things you’ve always taken for granted, we think you’ll leave seeing the human body a little differently.And this time come prepared with your questions!YOUR TICKETS INCLUDESA 45-minute live lectureAudience Q&AOne complimentary beer or mocktailABOUT THE SPEAKERDr Tanaya is a doctor, author, and sexual health educator who has spent years making conversations about bodies, sex, periods, pleasure, and reproductive health feel less shameful and more understandable for millions of people online. In this talk, she unpacks how bad anatomy, patriarchal science, cultural discomfort, and centuries of misinformation have shaped the way we think about sex, women’s orgasms, and why so many people still know so little about one of the body’s most important pleasure organs, or even where it is.From the clitoris’ surprisingly dramatic scientific history to the very real orgasm gap in heterosexual relationships, we’ll explore what defines good sex, whose pleasure gets prioritised, and Everything Nobody Tells You About Your Body - which also happens to be the title of her bestselling book.Disclaimer• By attending, you consent to being photographed and/or filmed. Event photos and videos may be used by Pint of View for promotional purposes.• Alcohol will not be served to attendees below 25 years. Your complimentary drink may be exchanged for a mocktail or another non-alcoholic beverage. Please carry a valid government-issued ID.

location.address: Eldeco Centre, Hauz Rani, Malviya Nagar, New Delhi, Delhi 110017, India

name: uL: Physics Across Frontiers
description: Join us for a lecture that takes you to the edges of what we think we know about reality, and shows you why the universe keeps proving us wrong.About the LectureOur intuition about the laws of nature comes from what we can see, touch, and measure in everyday life. Drop a ball, it falls. Push something, it moves. The world behaves, more or less, the way you'd expect.But physics has a habit of breaking its own rules.When objects travel close to the speed of light, time slows down. When things become impossibly small, they stop behaving like things at all. At every frontier — from Newton to Einstein, from Einstein to the architects of the Standard Model of Particle Physics — the greatest leaps in understanding have required scientists to abandon their most cherished assumptions and follow the data, however strange it looked.This lecture traces that journey across the frontiers of physics: the moments where intuition collided with reality. From the elegance of classical mechanics to the bizarre certainties of quantum physics, it asks what happens when the universe refuses to behave the way we think it should.The session explores:Why our everyday intuition about physics breaks down at extreme speeds and scalesHow Newton's laws gave way to Einstein's — and why Einstein's weren't enough eitherWhat the Standard Model of Particle Physics reveals about the deep structure of realityWhy every great leap in physics required abandoning assumptions, not just refining themThe frontier we stand at today — and why it may be the most challenging one yetAbout the SpeakerAbhishek Iyer's journey through physics took him from Fergusson College, Pune to IIT Kharagpur, and then to the Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore for a PhD — where most of his time was spent thinking about extra dimensions: the idea that space might hide more directions than we can perceive. He spent three years at the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research in Mumbai, two years at INFN Naples (also the birthplace of pizza), and continued at IP2I Lyon, the gastronomical capital of France, before arriving at IIT Delhi — still asking a similar set of questions.Fun fact: He is likely to be found at one of Delhi's many monuments on the weekends.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Lala And The Pursuit of Moolah
description: Join us for a lecture that asks a question most business textbooks skip entirely: what did Indian entrepreneurship look like centuries before the word "startup" existed?About the LectureIndian entrepreneurship remains strikingly under-represented in mainstream business literature. The balance tilted slightly with the government's enthusiasm for Startup India and its unicorns, but Indian entrepreneurship has a history stretching back hundreds of years before Zomato and Zerodha became household names. Most of us remain unaware of how Indian tradesmen and entrepreneurs innovated financial instruments, credit systems, accounting practices, and contract management long before these ideas appeared in Harvard textbooks — and of how much of that legacy has since faded into obscurity.About the SpeakerDr. Nihar Amoncar is Pro Vice Chancellor at IILM University and Director of the IILM School of Management. He completed his PhD in Family Business Strategy on a full scholarship from the University of South Wales, and has won several global awards for his research on traditional business communities, including the Best Paper Award at the Academy of Marketing. He is a Fellow of Higher Education (UK), serves on the Board of the Academy of Marketing's Special Interest Group on Entrepreneurship and Marketing, and is the Asia representative of GRCME (USA).PS: Your Ticket Includes a 250/- Coupon Redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from St. Stephen's, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. Each event is an invitation to spend an evening with an idea.

name: Haunted Walk: Mehrauli Archaeological Park
description: Join us for a hauntingly beautiful night walk through the Mehrauli Archaeological Park, a place where centuries of Delhi’s history come alive—alongside tales of the unseen. In this special segment, Ruhaani Raatein, we delve into the world of djinns, the mystical beings believed to reside in this ancient land.Guided by a historian and storyteller, we’ll explore how djinns are viewed and worshipped in Islamic traditions, and how folklore continues to shape the spiritual character of Mehrauli even today.Our walk will take you through: The tomb of Jamali Kamali, often called the Gay Taj Mahal for its poetic mystique and timeless love story A haunting 500-year-old baoli (stepwell)The remnants of a British-era swimming pool, hidden in plain sight A peaceful lakeside café, perfect for ending the evening with a quiet reflection or a warm chaiAs night falls and shadows grow, let the stories guide you through one of Delhi’s oldest, most magical spaces—where the past is never truly past.

name: The Listening Room
description: The Listening Room: A Space for Deep ListeningOverviewIn a world that is always talking, we rarely get the chance to truly listen. The Listening Room is a unique session dedicated to the art of being present. It is a space to share, to hear others, and to practice the kind of attention that builds real connection and empathy.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community led initiative dedicated to creating safe and mindful spaces. Through intimate workshops and gatherings, we help individuals navigate life’s complexities from mental well being to personal growth with empathy and shared human connection.What to expectActive Listening: Practice being fully present while others speak without the need to interrupt or give immediate advice.Shared Stories: A safe environment to share your own thoughts or experiences and feel truly heard by a small group.Quiet Reflection: Moments of silence to process what has been shared and to notice how deep listening changes our perspective.Supportive Connection: A morning at Greenr focused on human presence and the simple power of witnessing each other’s stories.Details🗓️ Date: 25th July (Saturday)⏰ Time: 11:00 AM📍 Location: Greenr, Golf Course Ext Road🎟️ Contribution: ₹700

name: The Epiloguers Club: Book Club Meet for July 2026
description: Last month, the world was the character. This month, we're looking at the creatures that inhabit it. 🐾July Theme: Creatures of FictionAnimals have always lived at the edges of our stories, and sometimes, right at the centre. This month we're reading fiction where animals are not decoration or symbolism, but a presence. Narrators, moral witnesses, companions, and protagonists who carry the weight of the story in ways no human character quite could.From a pig who understands power, to a sheep that solves mysteries, to a dog who loves unconditionally in a world that doesn't always deserve it : these are not small roles. These are the creatures that stay with you long after the book ends.Fiction, short stories, anthologies, all are welcome. No mythology, no fantasy creatures. Just animals, and the writers who gave them a story worth telling.Note: Please buy a beverage at the cafe on the day of the meet.Who are we?We are The Epiloguers Club, a book club for the people who after finishing a book crave to share their thoughts and invite discussion.Our name comes from the epilogue, that final, quiet space at the end of a story where loose ends are gathered and story settles in with its sigh or boom. We live in that space. We pull it apart, debate it, and keep it alive long after the book is closed.Our meets follow a bring-your-own-book format built around a conceptual theme, which means you arrive with one book and usually leave with twelve recommendations and at least three new interpretations of your own.You can follow us on Instagram for more updates: @the_epiloguers_club

name: The Cartographic Paradox
description: Join us for a lecture and workshop that begins with a deceptively simple question:What if the best way to understand a story is to draw a map of it?About the LectureFrom Treasure Island to Middle-earth, maps have lived at the heart of literature. We unfold them at the start of novels, trace routes with our fingers, lose ourselves in coastlines that don't exist. They feel like companions to the written word — helpful, decorative, secondary.But maps are far more than that. They are powerful narrative artefacts in their own right.Maps appear to tell the truth. They reassure us that the world is real, ordered, knowable. Yet every map is also an act of imagination — compressing vast territories into symbols, erasing details, privileging one perspective over another, transforming landscapes into stories.This lecture-cum-workshop explores the rich, tangled relationship between maps and literary imagination. Together, we'll journey through some of literature's most memorable cartographies, asking how maps shape our reading, and what they reveal about the worlds — both real and invented — that we inhabit.But the exploration doesn't stop at fiction. If stories can be mapped, can lives? What would a cartography of memory look like? What hidden regions of yourself might emerge if you treated identity not as a fixed point, but as a landscape?The session explores:Why maps have held such enduring power in literary culture — from adventure fiction to memoirHow maps function as texts that can be "read" for narrative, ideology, and desireWhat happens when we map not territories but experiences, memories, and identitiesThe creative potential of cartography as a tool for self-discovery and world-buildingPart lecture, part creative workshop — this is a session built for curiosity, reflection, and imaginative play. Through discussion and hands-on exercises, we'll experiment with mapping as a method of storytelling, narrative design, and understanding who we are.Welcome, cartographer.PS. Every ticket includes a 300/- coupon redeemable on F&B.About the SpeakerVaibhav Dwivedi is an Assistant Professor of English at St. Stephen's College, University of Delhi. He is currently pursuing his PhD at the University of Delhi, working on the intersection of fantastic literature and the house as an architectural and narrative space. His research interests include literature of the weird, cartography, and narratology. Alongside his academic work, he is engaged with theatre and photography — practices that inform and enrich his teaching.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: uL: The Secret History of Indian Sci-Fi
description: Join us for a lecture that uncovers a literary tradition most people don't know exists:India's own history of science fiction.About the LectureThere's a popular belief that science fiction is a Western genre — born in the laboratories and imaginations of Europe and America, with no real history or roots in India.But from the late nineteenth century, Indian writers were already using the tools of science fiction to grapple with the forces reshaping their world: industrialisation, colonialism, modernity, and the promise — and threat — of technology. This lecture traces that long, fascinating, and largely forgotten history. It explores how Indian writers adapted science fiction to the specificities of their world, and asks what it means that an entire literary tradition has been hiding in plain sight.The session explores:Why India's science fiction history has been overlooked — and what that erasure tells usThe pioneering speculative writing of Bose, Hossain, and other early Indian authorsHow post-Independence writers brought Indian scientific culture into the genreThe ways contemporary Indian sci-fi engages with themes from caste to climate to AIThis is a talk for anyone who loves stories about the future — and wants to discover that India has been writing them for longer than almost anyone realises.About the SpeakerGautam Bhatia is a writer, reviewer, and editor based in New Delhi. He is the author of The Sentence, and the science fiction duology The Wall and The Horizon, and serves as coordinating editor of the science fiction magazine Strange Horizons. In his other life, he is a constitutional lawyer and public commentator on civil and constitutional rights in India.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Festivals of Shahjahanabad by Rana Safvi
description: Kathika is delighted to host a special edition of Baithak Pe Charcha with celebrated author and historian Rana Safvi, as she takes audiences on a captivating journey through the vibrant festivals and seasonal traditions of Shahjahanabad. Set against the intimate, heritage-rich backdrop of Kathika’s Haveli, this interactive baithak, "Festivals of Shahjahanabad: Revisiting Phool Walon Ki Sair and Other Seasonal Celebrations," will rediscover the shared cultural heritage that once defined the heart of Old Delhi.Through fascinating anecdotes, historical accounts and personal reflections, eminent historian Rana Safvi will revisit the story of Phool Walon Ki Sair and other cherished seasonal celebrations that brought together communities across faiths and traditions. Audiences will gain a deeper understanding of how these festivals shaped the social and cultural life of Shahjahanabad, preserving its spirit of harmony, celebration, and collective memory. This promises to be an enriching evening of storytelling, history, and conversation that brings the city's living heritage vividly to life.

name: Emotion, Memory & Decision-Making Across Species
description: Join us for a lecture that asks a question stranger than it first sounds: can a fish feel, remember, and decide the way you do?About the LectureEmotions and memories guide our decisions so that we remain safe, healthy, and able to pursue long-term goals. Fish and rodents are widely used as animal models to understand clinical disorders marked by emotion and impulsivity — depression, addiction, suicide — yet a wide gap remains between animal and human studies. We don't actually know if a fish or a rodent can think, feel, or behave the way we do.In this talk, we ask whether emotion and memory shape decision-making across fish, rodents, and humans, how this question can be tested, and what some of the results reveal. It's interdisciplinary work aimed at improving clinical insight — and, perhaps, helping us understand ourselves and what might genuinely be unique about human behaviour.About the SpeakerDr. Varsha Singh is a Professor at the Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, working at the intersection of neuroscience, behaviour, and cognition. Her interdisciplinary research uses animal models to probe questions about emotion, memory, and decision-making that have direct bearing on how we understand human mental health and behaviour.PS: Your Ticket Includes a 250/- Coupon Redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from St. Stephen's, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. Each event is an invitation to spend an evening with an idea.

name: You Didn't Choose The Image; It's Deciding For You
description: Join us for a lecture that asks a deceptively simple question: who actually gets to decide what a hero, a victim, or a good life looks like?About the LectureWe don't choose the images that come to define us, and yet they decide more than we realise. A single gaze on a movie poster decides who counts as a hero. A familiar narrative arc decides who counts as a believable victim. A curated feed decides what a good life is supposed to look like.This talk moves through three of the speaker's own published studies — on Malayalam movie poster semiotics, on how Bollywood narratives construct the "ideal" rape victim, and on Instagram, loneliness, and FoMO among young Indians — to trace one quiet mechanism running underneath very different parts of Indian media. By the end, the question isn't really about posters, films, or apps. It's about who gets to build the image in the first place, and what we do with the gap between it and our own lives.About the SpeakerLydia Glory Jose is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Media and Journalism at IILM University, Gurugram, where she teaches media studies, visual communication, and research methods. Before academia, she worked as a journalist with Malayala Manorama, an experience that continues to shape how she reads Indian media — from the inside, not as an outsider looking in. Her research sits at the intersection of representation, visual semiotics, and digital media, published in Springer Nature's Sexuality and Culture, Palabra Clave, and the International Journal of Semiotics and Visual Rhetoric, and cited by the Times of India. Multilingual in Malayalam, Hindi, English, and Arabic, she remains, at heart, more interested in what an image is doing to us than in what it appears to be showing us.PS: Your Ticket Includes a 250/- Coupon Redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from St. Stephen's, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. Each event is an invitation to spend an evening with an idea.

name: uL: Badass Begums, The Women Of The Mughal Era
description: Join us for a lecture that steps into a side of Delhi's history that rarely makes it into textbooks: The women who built this city.About the LectureWhen we think of Mughal Delhi, we think of emperors. We think of conquest, succession, and the men whose names are carved into monuments. But behind — and often alongside — those names were formidable women whose influence shaped the city through architecture, politics, philanthropy, and patronage.They commissioned buildings. They brokered power. They funded entire neighbourhoods. And then, almost entirely, they were written out of the story.Drawing on rare paintings, forgotten anecdotes, and lesser-known archival stories, historian and author Anoushka Jain brings these women back into the history of the city they helped create.The session explores:Who the Mughal women behind Delhi's architecture and politics really wereHow begums and princesses wielded influence through patronage, charity, and urban designThe stories and images that have been overlooked or erased from mainstream historical narrativesWhat these legacies reveal about women, power, and public life in Mughal IndiaAbout the SpeakerAnoushka Jain is a historian, author, and founder of Enroute Indian History, a heritage initiative dedicated to uncovering and sharing India's lesser-known histories. She is the author of Badass Begums (HarperCollins India), a book that explores the lives of remarkable women who shaped the cultural and political landscape of Delhi.Her work has appeared in The Hindu, The Indian Express, Scroll.in, The Print, Hindustan Times, The Times of India, and Vogue India.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.
